@zakkster/lite-gradient-studio — LLM-targeted reference

# Identity

Authoring engine for OKLCH gradients. Linear, radial, conic, and N×M
mesh. Zero-GC rasterization, multi-format export, palette extraction.
Used in production by Gradient Studio (https://gradient.studio).

# Install

    npm install @zakkster/lite-gradient-studio

Deps install automatically: @zakkster/lite-color,
@zakkster/lite-color-engine, @zakkster/lite-gradient.

# Import

    import {
        Gradient,
        MeshGradient,
        formatCssLinear, formatCssRadial, formatCssConic, formatCssMesh,
        toTokens1d, toTokensMesh, EXPORT_FORMATS_1D, EXPORT_FORMATS_MESH, FORMAT_META,
        toHex, fromHex, oklchToLinearSrgb, linearSrgbToOklch,
        extractPalette,
        parseGradientCss,
        packOklchSingle, bakeGradientToLut, sampleLut, flattenStopsToBuffer,
        defaultMeshColor,
    } from '@zakkster/lite-gradient-studio';

# Most common patterns

## Sample a 1D gradient

    const g = new Gradient([
        { l: 0.4, c: 0.2, h: 270, pos: 0 },
        { l: 0.8, c: 0.2, h:  60, pos: 1 },
    ]);
    const out = { l: 0, c: 0, h: 0, a: 1 };
    g.at(0.5, out);              // mutates out, no allocation

## Emit CSS

    formatCssLinear(g, { angle: 135, oklchInterp: true });
    formatCssRadial(g, { shape: 'circle', position: '50% 50%', oklchInterp: true });
    formatCssConic(g,  { from: 0, position: '50% 50%', oklchInterp: true });

`oklchInterp: true` adds `in oklch` to the gradient — modern browsers
do the interpolation in OKLCH space. Without the hint they lerp in sRGB.

## Build, deform, and rasterize a mesh

    const m = new MeshGradient(5, 5);
    m.setPoint(2, 2, 0.65, 0.26, 320);          // mutate L/C/H at (col=2, row=2)
    m.setPointPosition(0, 0, -0.1, -0.1);       // pull a corner outside [0,1]
    const buf = new Uint32Array(W * H);
    m.rasterizeDeformedTo(buf, W, H, { interpolation: 'smooth' });
    // buf is ARGB-packed sRGB. Wrap as ImageData:
    const img = new ImageData(new Uint8ClampedArray(buf.buffer), W, H);

`MeshGradient.sampleAt(u, v, out, mode)` is uv-on-REGULAR-grid: deformed
positions affect rasterization geometry but NOT the color basis. To
"resample colors at fractional positions of the original grid" you call
sampleAt and ignore the deformation. That's the right behavior when
resizing a mesh and preserving its theme.

Mesh interpolation modes (sampleAt's `mode` arg / rasterize's
`opts.interpolation`):
- 'bilinear' — bilinear OKLCH. Fastest. Facet-visible at cell borders. Default.
- 'smooth'   — bilinear with smoothstep ease. C¹ at seams.
- 'cubic'    — Catmull-Rom across cells. Smoothest, ~2.5× cost.

Legacy boolean accepted for sampleAt: `false` → bilinear, `true` → smooth.
Rasterize also accepts `opts.smooth: true` as a legacy alias.

## Monochrome mesh (v1.1.0)

Factory for meshes where every control point shares base c/h (or c=0 for
grayscale) and only L varies across the grid. Client-work-friendly:
never looks like an "AI-generated random gradient" because the palette
is fixed to one brand tone.

    import { monochromeMesh, formatCssMesh } from '@zakkster/lite-gradient-studio';

    // Brand-tinted mesh, L varies diagonally
    const m = monochromeMesh({ l: 0.5, c: 0.06, h: 245 }, 3, 3);

    // Or explicit direction + grayscale + printable-safe range
    const gm = monochromeMesh(
        { l: 0.5, c: 0, h: 0 },
        4, 4,
        { mode: 'grayscale', range: [0.05, 0.95], direction: 'radial' }
    );

    // Emit CSS without canvas
    element.style.background = formatCssMesh(m);

Options:
- mode:       'tinted' (default) | 'grayscale'
- range:      [lo, hi] with 0 <= lo < hi <= 1 (default [0, 1])
- direction:  'horizontal' | 'vertical' | 'diagonal' (default) | 'radial'

Directions:
- horizontal:  L varies left-to-right, uniform per row
- vertical:    L varies top-to-bottom, uniform per column
- diagonal:    top-left corner (lo) to bottom-right corner (hi)
- radial:      center (lo) outward to corners (hi)

Returns a MeshGradient. All post-construction methods (setPointPosition,
setPoint, sampleAt, rasterizeTo, etc.) work as normal — the factory just
initializes the color basis. Deform handles off-grid to warp L across
the surface without disturbing the brand palette.

Pairs with @zakkster/lite-gradient's monochromeGradient(base, opts) which
returns a continuous 1D Gradient; monochromeMesh returns a 2D MeshGradient.

## Multi-format export

    const state = { mode: 'linear', angle: 135, stops };
    toTokens1d(state, 'css',      { name: 'sunset' });
    toTokens1d(state, 'tailwind', { name: 'sunset' });
    toTokens1d(state, 'svg',      { name: 'sunset' });

Signature: toTokens1d(g1dState, formatId, opts) — STATE first, FORMAT
second. Returns a string. toTokensMesh(mesh, formatId, opts) follows
the same pattern.

Supported 1D formats: 'css', 'css-var', 'scss', 'tailwind', 'json', 'svg'.
Supported mesh formats: 'css', 'css-var', 'json'.

## Parse a gradient CSS string

    const parsed = parseGradientCss('linear-gradient(135deg, oklch(0.4 0.2 270) 0%, oklch(0.8 0.2 60) 100%)');
    // → { mode: 'linear', angle: 135, stops: [{ l, c, h, pos }, ...] }

Round-trips with formatCssLinear/Radial/Conic.

## Extract a palette from an image

    // rgba is a Uint8ClampedArray of length width*height*4
    const palette = extractPalette(rgba, 5);
    // → [{ l, c, h }, ...] — up to 5 picks, chroma-weighted, ≥50° hue separated

Signature: extractPalette(pixels, count?). Width/height NOT required —
the algorithm scans linearly. `count` defaults to 5; output may be
shorter if the image lacks that many distinct hues.

Chroma-weighted means vivid colors win over dominant-by-pixel-count
colors. A baby-in-blue-shirt photo returns a palette with blue, not
five shades of skin.

## Convert OKLCH ↔ hex

    toHex({ l: 0.65, c: 0.26, h: 320 });     // → '#cf5fb9' (sRGB-gamut clipped)
    fromHex('#cf5fb9');                       // → { l, c, h, a }

# Stop shape

1D stops are { l, c, h, pos, a? }:
- l: OKLCH lightness, 0..1
- c: OKLCH chroma, 0..~0.4 (in-gamut for sRGB usually <0.30)
- h: OKLCH hue, degrees 0..360
- pos: position on the gradient, 0..1
- a: alpha, 0..1, defaults to 1

Mesh stops are { l, c, h, a?, x?, y? }:
- Same color fields as 1D
- x, y: deformed position in [0,1] (or outside). Optional in the
  constructor — defaults to the regular-grid layout (col / (cols-1),
  row / (rows-1)).

# Concurrency

Stateless. All operations are pure given inputs. No globals, no module
state. Concurrent rasterizes to different buffers are safe; concurrent
mutations of the same MeshGradient are NOT (no locking — caller's job).

# Performance

Benchmarks at npm run bench. Recent numbers (Node v22, Linux x64):
- Gradient.at: ~9M ops/sec, ~30M when called in a tight sweep
- MeshGradient.sampleAt (5×5 smooth): ~5M ops/sec
- rasterizeTo / rasterizeDeformedTo (5×5 → 256×256): ~2M px/sec
- formatCssLinear: ~360K ops/sec
- extractPalette (240×240 RGBA, k=5): ~41 calls/sec

Hot-path zero-GC pattern: pre-allocate Uint32Array buffers and scratch
{ l, c, h, a } objects, reuse across frames. `new ImageData(new
Uint8ClampedArray(buf.buffer), W, H)` wraps the same backing memory
the rasterizer writes — no copy on putImageData.

# Anti-patterns

- DO NOT construct a new Gradient per frame in a hot loop. Make it once,
  store the reference, mutate via setPoint / setPointPosition if
  the colors change.
- DO NOT pass a fresh scratch object to sampleAt per call. Allocate
  one at module scope or per editor instance.
- DO NOT trust `gradient.stops` to be sorted post-mutation. The 1D
  Gradient sorts on construction; if you build a stop array, sort by
  `pos` ascending before constructing.
- DO NOT rasterize at retina resolution every frame during a drag.
  Use a quarter-res offscreen canvas during drag, full-res on release.
  See Gradient Studio's mesh/render.js for the pattern.

# Why this library exists

The web has settled on OKLCH for color. The CSS Color Level 4 spec
adds the `in oklch` interpolation hint to gradients. Browsers shipped
oklch() in 2023. But editor-grade tooling — the layer above raw color
math, where you author multi-stop gradients with handles, deform a
mesh, and export to whichever target your downstream needs — is still
mostly stuck on HSL or sRGB.

This library is that layer. Built once, factored to compose, tuned for
production rasterization. Pair it with @zakkster/lite-color (the OKLCH
foundation) and any UI framework to ship a gradient editor.

## v1.2.0 "Tiles" — toroidal mesh

`new MeshGradient(cols, rows, stops, { wrapX, wrapY })` — 4th opts arg with two independent flags (torus = both, cylinder = one). Both default `false`; when off, all code paths byte-identical to v1.1.0.

Structural wrap semantics:
- UV period: cell count is `cols` (not `cols - 1`) on wrapped axes; default control-point positions become `col / cols`.
- `sampleAt` on wrapped axes: `u = u - Math.floor(u)` replaces clamp; last-cell neighbour uses `(col + 1) % cols`. `sampleAt(0, v) === sampleAt(1, v)` exactly; any float is a valid coord (raw accumulating animation phase, negatives).
- Cubic mode + wrap = C¹ across the seam. `_sampleAtCubic` and `_cubicRow` use modulo neighbour indexing (`((col - 1) % cols + cols) % cols` and `(col + 2) % cols`) so Catmull-Rom reads real cells across the boundary. The flagship — no other mesh gradient lib does this.
- `rasterizeTo` on wrapped axes: `u = x / width` (drops the `- 1`). Pixel column 0 of the "next tile" lands at u ≡ 0, so `drawImage`-based tiling of the rasterized output butts perfectly.
- `rasterizeDeformedTo` throws `err.code = 'WRAP_DEFORMED_UNSUPPORTED'` on a wrapped mesh. Ghost-quad seam crossing is v1.3 work.
- `defaultMeshColor` accepts trailing `wrapX`, `wrapY` args. When set, hue advances a uniform `360/cols` per column (wrapX) or L switches to `cos(2π · rT)` and drift becomes sinusoidal (wrapY). Four-arg form byte-identical to v1.1.0.

Instance exposes `readonly wrapX: boolean`, `readonly wrapY: boolean` for downstream consumers.

Peer bumps: `@zakkster/lite-color-engine` `^1.5.0`, `@zakkster/lite-gradient` `^1.2.0`.

Cascade note: dither (D7/D8) slips clean to v1.2.0 with full gate. Both roadmap tracks (wrap D1-D6, dither D7-D8) ship together in 1.2.0.

## v1.2.0 "Tiles + Dither"

Blue-noise dithering on `MeshGradient.rasterizeTo` — D7/D8 from the Tiles roadmap, slipped intact from v1.2.0 per the cascade rule. No peer bumps (engine v1.5.0 already floor from v1.2.0).

Usage:
```
mesh.rasterizeTo(buf, W, H, { dither: true });
mesh.rasterizeTo(buf, W, H, { dither: true, interpolation: 'cubic' });
mesh.rasterizeTo(buf, W, H, { dither: true /* + wrap */ });
```

Semantics:
- Delegates to `@zakkster/lite-color-engine >= 1.5.0`'s `packOklchBufferToUint32Dithered` + `getBlueNoise64`. Tile is a shared `Uint8Array(4096)` (64×64), decoded once from a base64 blob at first use.
- Per-pixel: `noise01 = (tile[((y & 63) << 6) | (x & 63)] + 0.5) / 256` — pure bit ops, torus wrap via mask. Same noise01 shared across R/G/B (no chroma speckle). Alpha undithered.
- Contract: `noise01 = 0.5` reproduces the plain packer exactly. Consequence: dither adds at most ±1 per-channel deviation vs undithered.
- Branch resolved ONCE per rasterizeTo call — two loop bodies. `dither: false` (or absent) walks the exact v1.2.0 code path, byte-identical output.
- Not on `rasterizeDeformedTo` (v1.3 ghost-quad work).

New export: `packOklchSingleDithered(l, c, h, alpha, noise01)` — scalar-arg wrapper reusing the same Float32Array(3) scratch as `packOklchSingle`.

Empirical dither overhead (5×5 smooth, 256²): 2.1M → 1.6M px/s, ~24%. Recorded in bench.

Total tests: 269/269 (218 v1.1.0 + 31 wrap + 3 wrap-alloc + 15 dither + 2 dither-alloc). Byte-parity gate on the v1.1.0 tests still holds unmodified.
